defects: Section 1 (rejected-models, CRDT-gossip bullet) overstates the cross-region network reduction as '~5-6 orders of magnitude less.' Using the answer's own topology (~5 regions x 50-200 shards = 250-1000 total shards, each shipping absolute per-shard state at >=1/sec), the reduction from 1M raw events/sec is only ~1000-4000x, i.e. ~3 orders of magnitude. Reaching 5-6 OOM would require ~1-10 aggregate messages/sec, which contradicts the stated shard count at a per-second shipping cadence. Overstated by roughly 2-3 orders of magnitude.

Section 0 states cross-region consensus is 'roughly 10,000-100,000x short' of 1M/sec, but this is inconsistent with the answer's own stated ceiling of 7-20 commits/s: 1M/20 = 50,000x and 1M/7 = 142,857x, so the true shortfall is ~50,000x-143,000x. The '10,000x' low bound understates by ~5x. (The separately-stated '4-5 orders of magnitude' framing is correct at 4.7-5.2 OOM, making this a self-inconsistency in the answer's numbers.)

Radix section, point 2 ("The apparent speedup is paid for in a hidden parameter"): the sub-argument is mathematically self-contradictory and states a false conclusion. It correctly derives that choosing radix b ≈ n to sort integers in [0, n^c) gives d = O(c) passes and total work O(c·n), but then claims "c = Θ(log n / log b) when the universe is genuinely unbounded, so the general case lands back at Ω(n log n)." With b ≈ n we have log b = log n, so that very formula yields c = Θ(1) — a constant — giving O(n), NOT Ω(n log n). The standard CLRS result confirms radix sort runs n integers in [0, n^c) for constant c in Θ(cn) = O(n) time, which genuinely beats n log n. Verified: even the minimal 'n arbitrary distinct integers' case (universe ~n, w = ⌈log₂ n⌉ bits) sorts in O(n) via a single counting-sort pass with b ≈ n. So the claim that radix's actual cost 'lands back at Ω(n log n)' for polynomially-bounded universes is false — the Ω(n log n) figure only reappears under the comparison-SIMULATION accounting described in the separate 'consistency check' paragraph (which is correct), or when the universe is super-polynomial in n (c growing). Point 2 conflates radix's real RAM-model cost with the comparison-simulated cost.
